items.evidence_text:
Client: Hi Alex. Post-divorce, I’m managing everything solo and I just want a sanity check—like, make sure I’m not missing something obvious.
Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s keep it practical. First, big picture: what’s your household income these days—roughly per year?
Client: It’s rental income, and the annual gross is 220100.
Advisor: Got it—rental income annually. And on the spending side, what do your total monthly expenses usually run?
Client: 8600 a month, give or take a little depending on repairs.

Client: It’s rental income, and the annual gross is 220100.
Advisor: Got it—rental income annually. And on the spending side, what do your total monthly expenses usually run?
Client: 8600 a month, give or take a little depending on repairs.
Advisor: Okay. And do you have any monthly debt payments—mortgage, car loan, credit cards—anything like that?
Client: No, monthly debt payments are 0.

Client: Hi Alex. Post-divorce, I’m managing everything solo and I just want a sanity check—like, make sure I’m not missing something obvious.
Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s keep it practical. First, big picture: what’s your household income these days—roughly per year?
Client: It’s rental income, and the annual gross is 220100.

Client: 8600 a month, give or take a little depending on repairs.

Client: No, monthly debt payments are 0.
Advisor: Great. One sec—just noting that down. Now I want to understand what you’ve got working for you. About how much do you have in investable assets total?
Client: Total investable assets are 513050.
Advisor: Thanks. Let’s break that down so I’m not guessing. Do you have anything held with an insurance company—like a taxable account or policy cash value?
Client: Yes—there’s a taxable account held at an insurance company, and it’s 279850.

Client: Yes, private markets held at a bank—73350.
Advisor: And you mentioned you’re on your own now—do you own a home, and if so what do you estimate it’s worth?
Client: Yes, primary residence held on an advisor platform—156850.
Advisor: Okay, that helps. On the liability side, just confirming: no loans outstanding at all right now?
Client: Correct—no loans outstanding.

Advisor: Hi Mar—good to meet you. I’m Alex. Before we dig into numbers, what made you decide to come in now?
Client: Hi Alex. Post-divorce, I’m managing everything solo and I just want a sanity check—like, make sure I’m not missing something obvious.
Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s keep it practical. First, big picture: what’s your household income these days—roughly per year?
Client: It’s rental income, and the annual gross is 220100.

Client: Yes—1 adult.
Advisor: Got it. Now taxes—when you think about your marginal bracket, where are you landing these days?
Client: 32%.
Advisor: Okay. And that rental income you mentioned earlier—the 220100 annually—puts you there pretty consistently, or does it swing a lot year to year?
Client: It’s pretty consistent. I mean it can move a bit, but the annual rental income is 220100.

Client: Hi Alex. Post-divorce, I’m managing everything solo and I just want a sanity check—like, make sure I’m not missing something obvious.
Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s keep it practical. First, big picture: what’s your household income these days—roughly per year?
Client: It’s rental income, and the annual gross is 220100.
Advisor: Got it—rental income annually. And on the spending side, what do your total monthly expenses usually run?
Client: 8600 a month, give or take a little depending on repairs.

Advisor: Perfect. Liabilities we already nailed down as zero—no mortgage, no other debt—and protection policies are none right now.
Client: Correct.
Advisor: Last admin thing, Mar—how do you want to engage with me going forward? Are you specifically looking for an RIA relationship, meaning a registered investment advisor, versus like a brokerage setup?
Client: Yes—RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Great, that helps me frame how we’ll work together and what you’ll expect from me.

Advisor: Okay. And what state are you a resident of right now? Just the two-letter is fine.
Client: IL.
Advisor: Perfect. Now, zooming out a bit—when you think about how you want this invested, what’s your risk comfort level? Like conservative, moderate, aggressive—what fits you best?
Client: Conservative. I’m conservative.
Advisor: Makes sense, especially given what you said earlier about wanting to avoid avoidable mistakes.

Client: Correct—no loans outstanding.
Advisor: And protection—any life insurance, disability coverage, anything like that we should factor in?

Advisor: Okay—thanks, Mar. I’ve got the accounts and the no-debt, no-insurance picture. Let’s grab a couple household basics so I don’t make assumptions.
Advisor: First, marital status for planning purposes—are you currently married, partnered, single… what should I put down?
Client: Divorced. Definitely divorced.
Advisor: Got it. And do you have any dependents—kids or anyone you financially support day-to-day?
Client: No—0 dependents.

Client: Yes, gross annual income is 220100, from rental income.

Client: Finance. I work in finance—well, around it, but yeah, finance.

Client: Yeah, I’m fine with the details—I just don’t want to miss something obvious.

Client: Sure.
Advisor: On your work status right now—are you currently working, between roles, retired, on leave… how would you describe it?
Client: I’m inactive.
Advisor: Got it—inactive. When you say inactive, is that like not employed at the moment, or more like you’re not actively working but you still have the rental income coming in?
Client: Not employed at the moment. The rentals are still producing income, but I’m personally inactive.

Advisor: Great—thank you. Next time we’ll talk through what “conservative” means in practice for your mix, and whether any coverage makes sense, but we’re in a good spot for today.

Advisor: You’re “Client 1,” right?
Client: Yes—Client 1.
Advisor: Great. And in the household, you’re the primary person we’re planning around—so your role is primary, correct?
